food_subgroup: Enriched soft bread with savoury topping

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for floss bun; closest parent 'bread product' (FOODON_03302397) used as anchor.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A000F 'Bread and similar products' is the closest defensible parent; no specific code for floss-topped soft bun exists in FoodEx2.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: SNOMED CT 226529007 'Bread' used as closest parent; no specific concept for floss bun exists.

serving.natural_language_portion: One floss bun typically weighs approximately 80 g and is served as a single-piece bakery item at Singaporean kaya toast and coffee shop chains.

nutrient_source.title: Soft bread roll with meat topping — composite estimate derived from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entries: Bread, white, commercially prepared (FDC ID 325871) and Pork, cured, meat floss (rousong) analogue via USDA FDC ID 168168 (pork, cured, cooked), adjusted for typical floss bun composition (approximately 75% enriched white bread dough, 15% pork floss, 10% mayonnaise/butter dressing) as documented in peer-reviewed bakery composition literature.
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service — FoodData Central SR Legacy
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No exact entry for floss bun exists in HPB Singapore Foodo or USDA FoodData Central. Values are estimated from a weighted composite of USDA SR Legacy white bread (FDC 325871), pork floss analogue (high-protein dried pork product, FDC 168168), and mayonnaise (FDC 172741) at proportions consistent with standard Singaporean bakery floss bun formulation (~75 g bread base per 100 g product, ~15 g pork floss, ~10 g mayonnaise/butter). Energy cross-checked against HPB Singapore nutrient reference ranges for enriched bread products.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values 2021 — white bread and enriched soft bread roll analogues
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson FS et al., Diabetes Care 2021
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Singaporean floss bun. GI of 70 is assigned based on the closest analogous food: white bread roll / enriched soft white bread (GI ~70–72, glucose reference) as listed in the University of Sydney GI Database and confirmed in Atkinson et al. 2021 international GI tables. The bread component constitutes approximately 75% of the product by weight; the pork floss and mayonnaise topping contribute negligible additional glycemic load. GL per serving calculated as (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (70 × 37.2) / 100 ≈ 26.0, where carbohydrate per 80 g serving = 46.5 × 0.80 = 37.2 g.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content (~520 mg per 100 g; ~416 mg per 80 g serving) from seasoned pork floss and bread dough
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit floss bun consumption due to its high sodium content (~416 mg per serving), which represents approximately 18% of the recommended daily sodium limit of 2300 mg; choosing lower-sodium bread alternatives or reducing portion size is advisable.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High glycemic index (~70) and high glycemic load (~26 per serving) from refined white bread base
type2_diabetes.guidance: Individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should consume floss bun with caution given its high GI (~70) and high GL (~26 per 80 g serving), which can cause rapid postprandial blood glucose elevation; pairing with a protein or fibre source and limiting to half a bun may help moderate glycaemic response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Moderate saturated fat (~2.9 g per 100 g) and elevated sodium from pork floss and mayonnaise dressing
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should be mindful that floss bun contributes moderate saturated fat and elevated sodium per serving; occasional consumption as part of a diet otherwise low in saturated fat and sodium is acceptable, but regular daily intake is not recommended.

llm_training.natural_language_description: A floss bun (肉松面包, rou song mian bao) is a popular Singaporean and East Asian bakery item consisting of a soft, slightly sweet enriched white bread roll generously topped with pork floss (rousong) — a light, airy, fibrous dried meat product made from slow-cooked and shredded pork seasoned with soy sauce, sugar, and spices — bound to the bun surface with a thin layer of mayonnaise or butter. The bread base is pillowy and mildly sweet, while the pork floss topping provides a savoury, umami-rich, slightly salty contrast with a melt-in-the-mouth cottony texture. Floss buns are a staple at Singaporean kaya toast chains such as Toast Box and Ya Kun, as well as at Chinese bakeries across Singapore, Malaysia, Taiwan, and mainland China. They are commonly consumed as a breakfast item or afternoon snack alongside kopi (local coffee) or teh (local tea). From a nutritional standpoint, the floss bun is energy-dense (~318 kcal per 100 g), high in refined carbohydrates (GI ~70), and moderately high in sodium (~520 mg per 100 g), making it a food that individuals with Type 2 Diabetes, hypertension, or cardiovascular disease should consume in moderation. The protein content (~11 g per 100 g) is relatively higher than plain white bread due to the pork floss component. Chicken floss variants are also available and are considered halal-friendly when certified.
common_user_queries: How many calories in a floss bun?
Is floss bun suitable for diabetics?
Is floss bun high in sodium?
Can I eat floss bun if I have high blood pressure?

confidence_reason: No exact entry for floss bun exists in HPB Singapore Foodo or any primary regional nutrition database. Nutrient values are estimated from a weighted composite of USDA SR Legacy entries for white bread, pork floss analogue, and mayonnaise at proportions consistent with standard Singaporean bakery formulation. GI is assigned by analogy to enriched white bread roll from the University of Sydney GI Database. Confidence is therefore medium rather than high.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are composite estimates; an exact HPB Singapore Foodo or ICMR-NIN entry for floss bun was not located as of the processing date. Values should be updated if an exact primary-source entry becomes available. 2. vitamin_a_mcg_rae is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA SR Legacy white bread entry and is not reliably estimable for the composite. 3. vitamin_b6_mg is -1 as it is not reported consistently across the composite source entries. 4. vitamin_b12_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA SR Legacy white bread entry; trace amounts may be present from pork floss but are not quantified. 5. folate_mcg_dfe is -1 as it is not reported in the composite source entries used. 6. vitamin_d_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the source database entries. 7. vitamin_e_mg is -1 as it is not reported in the composite source entries. 8. selenium_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the composite source entries. 9. vitamin_c_mg is 0.0; confirmed zero as white bread and pork floss contain no meaningful vitamin C; confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entries. 10. Halal status is marked false as the standard product uses pork floss; chicken floss variants sold at halal-certified outlets would carry a different halal status. 11. GI glycemic load per serving calculated as (70 × 37.2) / 100 = 26.0, where 37.2 g carbohydrate is derived from 46.5 g/100 g × 80 g serving size.
